treadmill.symptom: Belt slows or stops under load, hesitates when stepped on, burning smell from friction
treadmill.root_cause: Walking belt and/or deck surface worn out, creating excess friction. Accounts for 85% of treadmill power-loss complaints per Treadmill Doctor data
treadmill.notes: First thing to check on ANY power-loss complaint. Lubricate every 40 hours or 3 months. Lift belt edge — if deck surface feels rough or dry, lubricate with 100% silicone. If belt underside is shiny/smooth, replace belt. Deck replacement: $200-250 for part alone.

treadmill.symptom: Burning smell at higher speeds, motor sparking visible through vents, intermittent power loss, treadmill struggles under load
treadmill.root_cause: Carbon brush wear from normal use, accelerated by dust buildup. Brushes are consumable contact points inside DC motors
treadmill.notes: NUMBER ONE MISDIAGNOSIS in the industry. Shops frequently replace the entire motor ($200-600+) when $15 brushes fix it. Replace every 10 years or 1,000 hours. If new brushes wear out fast, THEN motor replacement is warranted. Clean brush area with sandpaper if lightly worn. Accounts for roughly 5% of power-loss issues but is the most over-repaired component.

treadmill.symptom: Belt slips when running or walking, belt drifts left or right, belt feels loose underfoot
treadmill.root_cause: Belt tension too loose (stretches over time) or rear roller misaligned causing drift. Over-tightening causes premature bearing and motor wear
treadmill.notes: Tighten rear roller bolts 1/4 turn at a time on each side. Belt should lift 2-3 inches from deck center. For tracking: tighten the bolt on the side the belt drifts TOWARD by 1/4 turn. Always lubricate before tightening — 8% of power-loss issues are from over-tight belt/motor belt.

treadmill.symptom: Treadmill powers on but belt won't move, erratic speed changes, complete power failure, burning electronics smell, error codes on display
treadmill.root_cause: Power surge damage, capacitor failure, MOSFET burnout, or loose wire connections on the MCB. Heat buildup from dust accelerates failure
treadmill.notes: Accounts for roughly 2% of power-loss issues but is expensive. Check for visibly blown capacitors (bulging tops) or burned components on the board. Always use a surge protector — power surges are the #1 cause. A live console with non-spinning belt often points here. Test voltage output to motor before condemning the board.

treadmill.symptom: Incline runs up/down continuously without stopping, incline won't respond to controls, incline calibration error codes, grinding noise during incline change
treadmill.root_cause: Incline motor burnout, damaged wiring between deck and lift motor, or calibration drift requiring recalibration procedure
treadmill.notes: Most common symptom is continuous incline cycling. First try recalibration procedure (varies by brand — check manual). Check the wire harness between deck and motor for pinching or damage before replacing the motor. NordicTrack/ProForm units especially prone to calibration drift.

treadmill.symptom: Console completely dead, display flickers or is unresponsive, touchscreen frozen, iFIT software errors or retry loops
treadmill.root_cause: Loose wiring between console and controller, failed power board inside console, software corruption (iFIT-equipped models), or blown internal fuse
treadmill.notes: First: unplug for 10 minutes to reset. Second: check 8-pin connector between controller and console. Third: check reset circuit breaker near power cord inlet. NordicTrack/ProForm iFIT models frequently need software updates or factory resets. Pinched wire during assembly is surprisingly common. Many consoles use standard tablet hardware internally.

treadmill.symptom: Erratic speed readings, speed display jumps randomly, E05 or similar error codes, console shows speed but belt isn't matching
treadmill.root_cause: Speed sensor (reed switch or optical) misaligned from front roller magnet, sensor failure, or debris between sensor and magnet
treadmill.notes: Often misdiagnosed as controller board failure. Sensor is typically mounted near the front roller. Clean the sensor and magnet, check gap distance (usually 1/8 inch). E05 error code is almost always this. Reed switch type sensors can be tested with a multimeter for continuity when magnet passes.

treadmill.symptom: Grinding or rumbling noise from front or rear roller area, vibration felt through deck, belt tracking issues that won't resolve with adjustment
treadmill.root_cause: Bearing wear from age, dust infiltration, or lack of lubrication. Front roller bearings fail more often due to motor drive stress
treadmill.estimated_repair_cost: $20-50 bearings DIY, $80-200 roller assembly, $150-300 with labor
treadmill.notes: Bearings are standard sizes available at bearing supply houses for much less than OEM roller assemblies. Remove roller, press out old bearing, press in new. Front roller bearings take more abuse. If roller crown (slight barrel shape) is worn flat, replace entire roller.

treadmill.symptom: Squealing noise from motor area, belt slips at higher speeds or under load, visible cracking or glazing on belt surface
treadmill.root_cause: Drive belt (connects motor to front roller) stretching, glazing, or cracking from heat and age
treadmill.notes: Often confused with walking belt slip. Drive belt is inside the motor housing connecting motor shaft to front roller. Glazed belt (shiny surface) slips under load. Check tension — should deflect about 1/2 inch with moderate pressure. Replace rather than attempting to de-glaze.

treadmill.symptom: Treadmill won't start at all, intermittently shuts off mid-use, safety key appears properly seated but no response
treadmill.root_cause: Reed switch or magnetic switch behind safety key slot worn or failed, safety key magnet weakened, or corroded contacts
treadmill.estimated_repair_cost: $5-15 DIY (replacement key), $10-30 for switch, $80-120 with labor
treadmill.notes: Very common and very cheap fix that gets overlooked. Try a different safety key first (they're universal magnetic type). If key is fine, the reed switch behind the console slot has failed. Can test with multimeter. This is a SAFETY device — never bypass it permanently.

treadmill.symptom: Treadmill completely dead, intermittent power cuts especially when cord is moved, visible damage or burn marks at power inlet
treadmill.root_cause: Power cord internal break from repeated flexing (especially if treadmill is moved), or inlet receptacle solder joints cracked from vibration
treadmill.notes: SAFETY CHECK — inspect for burn marks, melting, or arcing at inlet. This is a fire hazard if damaged. Check cord by wiggling at inlet while machine is on — if power flickers, replace immediately. IEC C14 inlets are standard and cheap. Always check this before diagnosing deeper electrical issues.

elliptical.symptom: Squeaking, clicking, or popping sounds during stride, especially at top or bottom of motion. Noise worsens over time
elliptical.root_cause: Sealed bearing wear in pivot points of stride/linkage arms from repetitive motion and sweat exposure
elliptical.notes: Most common elliptical complaint. Apply silicone lubricant to all pivot points first as a quick fix. If noise persists, bearings need replacement. Standard sealed bearings are cheap but access can require significant disassembly. Keep pivot bolts properly torqued.

elliptical.symptom: Resistance won't change when adjusted, resistance stuck at one level, no resistance at all, motor buzzing but resistance not responding
elliptical.root_cause: Resistance motor (servo or eddy current brake) failure, wiring harness damage, or control board signal loss
elliptical.notes: If motor receives voltage but doesn't move, replace the motor. If no voltage reaches motor, check wiring harness and console control board. Some models use a small servo motor that physically moves the magnet bracket — these fail more often than eddy current systems.

elliptical.symptom: Clicking or grinding noise from pedal area during each stride, pedal feels loose or wobbly, lateral play in pedal
elliptical.root_cause: Pedal arm bearing wear, often accelerated by user weight and lateral forces during exercise
elliptical.notes: Pedal bearings take the most abuse on an elliptical. Tighten all pedal arm bolts first — loose hardware mimics bearing failure. Replace bearings in pairs (both sides). Some models use bushings instead of bearings — these wear faster.

elliptical.symptom: Console completely dead, display flickers, buttons unresponsive, console powers on but shows no data
elliptical.root_cause: Failed power supply to console, loose wiring harness between frame and console mast, or internal board failure
elliptical.notes: Wires running through the mast tube to the console get pinched or disconnected — check this first. Elliptical consoles take less electrical abuse than treadmill consoles since there's no motor controller. Try unplugging for 10 minutes to reset. Check if console is powered by batteries or AC adapter.

elliptical.symptom: Grinding, humming, or whirring noise that increases with pedal speed, coming from the flywheel housing area
elliptical.root_cause: Flywheel shaft bearing wear from age and dust contamination
elliptical.notes: Flywheel bearings are standard sealed bearings. Access requires removing side covers and the flywheel itself. Replace both bearings even if only one is noisy. Use a bearing puller for removal — hammering can damage the shaft.

elliptical.symptom: Slipping sensation during stride, squealing noise from flywheel area, visible cracking or fraying on belt
elliptical.root_cause: Drive belt wear from normal use, belt stretching beyond adjustment range, or belt glazing from heat
elliptical.notes: Multi-ribbed belts are common on ellipticals. Check for cracks, missing ribs, or glazed surface. Tension adjustment varies by model. Replace with exact OEM spec — width and rib count must match. Dust and debris on pulleys accelerate belt wear.

elliptical.symptom: Stride length stuck at one setting, power stride adjustment motor not responding, clunking when stride changes
elliptical.root_cause: Motorized stride mechanism seized, track rails corroded or debris-bound, or adjustment motor failure
elliptical.notes: Only applies to models with adjustable stride length. Clean and lubricate the stride adjustment rails with silicone spray first. If motorized, check wiring to adjustment motor. Manual adjustment types can seize from sweat corrosion on the adjustment pin mechanism.

elliptical.symptom: Loud clunk once per revolution, visible wobble in crank arm, pedal arm feels like it shifts laterally during stride
elliptical.root_cause: Crank arm bolt loosening over time, worn splines on crank arm or axle, or bent axle from impact
elliptical.estimated_repair_cost: $0 (retorque bolt), $40-120 crank arm, $80-200 axle with labor
elliptical.notes: Retorque crank arm bolts periodically — this is the #1 preventable elliptical issue. Once splines are wallowed out from riding on a loose crank arm, the entire arm must be replaced. Use a torque wrench to the manufacturer specification.

elliptical.symptom: Thumping or bumping sensation during stride, visible flat spots on guide rollers, plastic debris near roller tracks
elliptical.root_cause: Guide roller wheels developing flat spots from sitting in one position for extended periods, or general wear
elliptical.estimated_repair_cost: $30-60 per wheel set DIY, $120-200 with labor
elliptical.notes: Wheels are typically about $40 each. Replace in full sets. Flat spots develop when machine sits unused — periodically move the pedals to a different resting position. Clean the guide rails regularly to reduce wheel wear.

stair_stepper.symptom: No resistance regardless of level setting, resistance fluctuates unpredictably, burning smell from alternator housing
stair_stepper.root_cause: Alternator brush wear (similar to treadmill motor brushes), bearing failure, or winding damage in the resistance generator
stair_stepper.estimated_repair_cost: $30-60 brush kit DIY, $150-400 alternator replacement with labor
stair_stepper.notes: Alternator brush kits are available and are the first thing to check — same principle as treadmill motor brushes. If brushes are good, check wiring to the console for signal integrity. Full alternator replacement is expensive but usually not needed if brushes are maintained.

stair_stepper.symptom: Clicking or grinding noise during stepping, chain visibly slack, steps feel uneven or jerky, chain jumping sprockets
stair_stepper.root_cause: Chain stretch from normal use (commercial revolving staircase units), worn sprockets, or insufficient lubrication
stair_stepper.estimated_repair_cost: $100-300 chain, $200-500 with labor. Sprocket replacement additional $100-200
stair_stepper.notes: SAFETY: Worn chains can skip or break during use. Measure chain stretch — replace if links measure over spec. Lubricate with marine-grade grease via Zerk fittings per manufacturer schedule. Check sprocket teeth for hooking (shark-fin shape means replacement needed). Commercial stepmills need chain inspection monthly.

stair_stepper.symptom: One or both pedals sink without resistance, oil visible around cylinder, pedal won't return to top position
stair_stepper.root_cause: Hydraulic cylinder seal failure allowing fluid leak, loss of hydraulic fluid pressure
stair_stepper.notes: Home-use hydraulic steppers are budget equipment and cylinders are considered consumable. Replacement is straightforward — usually two bolts per cylinder. Buy from manufacturer for correct stroke length and resistance rating. Upgrade to a magnetic resistance stepper if cylinders fail repeatedly.

stair_stepper.symptom: Console dead, display shows garbled data, step count inaccurate, buttons unresponsive
stair_stepper.root_cause: Wiring harness damage from vibration, battery corrosion (battery-powered consoles), or main board failure
stair_stepper.notes: StairMaster consoles are proprietary and expensive. Check battery terminals for corrosion first (clean with vinegar on cotton swab). Verify wiring harness connections from base to console. Commercial units often have replaceable console modules.

stair_stepper.symptom: Steps don't reach full range of motion, bottom step hits frame, uneven step depth between left and right
stair_stepper.root_cause: Calibration drift from chain stretch, sensor misalignment, or control board losing stored calibration values
stair_stepper.notes: Most commercial stepmills have a calibration procedure accessible through service menus. Consult the service manual for the specific key sequence. If calibration won't hold, the position sensor or control board may need replacement. Chain adjustment often resolves this without electronic recalibration.

stair_stepper.symptom: Slipping sensation under load, squealing from drive area, reduced step resistance, visible belt wear
stair_stepper.root_cause: Drive belt wear from normal use, belt tension loss, or pulley misalignment
stair_stepper.notes: Check belt tension and alignment before replacing. Glazed surface (shiny) means belt is slipping and needs replacement. Tension springs can weaken over time requiring adjustment or replacement alongside the belt.

stair_stepper.symptom: Grinding noise from step mechanism, rough step motion, visible rust on step rods
stair_stepper.root_cause: Bearing wear and corrosion from sweat exposure, lack of lubrication schedule adherence
stair_stepper.notes: Commercial stepmills have many step rod bearings that need regular greasing. Marine grease applied via Zerk fittings per manufacturer schedule prevents most failures. Once bearings are gone, replacement is labor-intensive due to step disassembly requirements.

stair_stepper.symptom: Lateral wobble in pedals/steps, clunking sound at top or bottom of step stroke, visible play when pedal is pushed side to side
stair_stepper.root_cause: Bushing wear from repetitive motion, especially on independent-pedal stepper designs
stair_stepper.notes: Bushings are cheap but access varies by model. On home units, this is often the first thing to wear out. Inspect for metal-on-metal contact where bushing has worn through. Upgrade to bearing-equipped pivot if available for the model.

exercise_bike.symptom: No resistance at any level, resistance stuck at one level, uneven resistance during pedaling, squealing when resistance is applied
exercise_bike.root_cause: Magnetic brake misalignment, friction pad wear (felt/leather), eddy current brake wiring failure, or resistance control cable stretch
exercise_bike.estimated_repair_cost: $15-40 pads/cable DIY, $50-150 magnetic system, $100-250 with labor
exercise_bike.notes: Friction pad systems: replace felt pads when thin or glazed ($10-20). Magnetic systems: check magnet bracket position and control cable/motor. Eddy current: check wiring between console and brake. Keiser M3 uses magnetic with no contact parts — fewer wear issues. Recumbent bikes often use tension cable that stretches.

exercise_bike.symptom: Seat drops during riding, adjustment knob won't hold position, seat post stuck and won't adjust, creaking from seat area
exercise_bike.root_cause: Quick-release or pop-pin mechanism wear, corrosion on seat post from sweat, stripped adjustment threading
exercise_bike.notes: Prevention: grease the seat post during initial assembly. For stuck posts: penetrating oil (PB Blaster) and gentle twisting. Sweat is the #1 cause of corrosion. Pop-pin style adjustments are more reliable than knob/clamp designs. Seat rail sliders on recumbent bikes wear out faster than upright seat posts.

exercise_bike.symptom: Clicking once per revolution, grinding from pedal area, pedal wobbles on spindle, pedal loosening during use
exercise_bike.root_cause: Pedal bearing wear, loose pedal threads (note: left pedal is reverse-threaded), or worn crank arm threads
exercise_bike.estimated_repair_cost: $15-50 replacement pedals DIY, $30-100 for SPD/clipless, $80-150 with labor
exercise_bike.notes: CRITICAL: Left pedal is reverse-threaded (lefty-tighty, righty-loosey). This is the most common mistake leading to stripped crank arms. Most pedals are standard 9/16 thread. Pedals falling off is reportedly the most common exercise bike repair — nearly always from wrong-direction tightening on the left side.

exercise_bike.symptom: Console dead, no display, inaccurate readings, buttons unresponsive, Bluetooth/ANT+ connectivity lost
exercise_bike.root_cause: Dead batteries (many bikes use AA or AAA), loose wiring, reed switch misalignment (for RPM/speed), or board failure
exercise_bike.notes: Many budget bike consoles are battery-powered. Check batteries first. For self-generating consoles (powered by pedaling), the generator may have failed. Reed switch for speed sensing is mounted near the flywheel and can shift out of alignment. Sweat dripping into console is a common cause of intermittent failures.

exercise_bike.symptom: Grinding or whirring noise that increases with pedal speed, rough/gritty feel when pedaling, vibration through frame
exercise_bike.root_cause: Flywheel shaft bearing wear from age, dust infiltration, or moisture from sweat
exercise_bike.notes: Standard sealed bearings — note the bearing number stamped on the side and order exact replacement. Requires removing flywheel cover and flywheel. Use a bearing puller, never hammer directly. Replace both bearings even if only one is noisy. Heavier flywheels put more stress on bearings.

exercise_bike.symptom: Clunking once per pedal revolution, visible movement at crank-to-axle junction, pedal arm feels like it shifts
exercise_bike.root_cause: Crank arm bolt loosening, stripped splines on crank arm or bottom bracket axle, or crank arm fatigue crack
exercise_bike.estimated_repair_cost: $20-50 crank arm DIY, $40-80 with crank puller tool, $100-180 with labor
exercise_bike.notes: Retorque crank bolts monthly — this prevents most crank arm failures. Once splines are wallowed out from riding on a loose crank, the arm must be replaced. Peloton crank arm replacement is a well-documented DIY repair. A crank puller tool ($10-15) is required for removal.

exercise_bike.symptom: Slipping under hard pedaling, visible cracks or missing teeth on belt, chain noise or skipping, loss of power transfer
exercise_bike.root_cause: Belt stretch or wear (belt-drive), chain stretch or rust (chain-drive), or tension adjustment needed
exercise_bike.estimated_repair_cost: $20-50 belt or chain DIY, $80-150 with labor
exercise_bike.notes: Belt-drive bikes are lower maintenance than chain-drive. For belts: check for cracks, missing teeth, glazing. For chains: lubricate regularly, check for stiff links. Measure belt/chain length against spec — stretched beyond limit means replacement. Note exact length and width before ordering replacement.

exercise_bike.symptom: Creaking or clicking centered at the crank area (not pedals), rough rotation when cranks are spun by hand, lateral play in crank axle
exercise_bike.root_cause: Bottom bracket bearing wear from sweat dripping down seat post into bracket area, dust, and normal use
exercise_bike.notes: Bottom bracket bearings on exercise bikes see more sweat exposure than outdoor bicycles. Requires crank puller and bottom bracket tools for replacement. Many exercise bikes use standard bicycle bottom bracket sizes. Sweat guard installation can prevent premature failure.

rower.symptom: No resistance or inconsistent resistance, gritty feel during pull, damper not responding to setting changes
rower.root_cause: Water rowers: impeller vane damage or water level change. Air rowers: damper flap stuck or dirty. Magnetic: servo motor failure or wiring issue
rower.estimated_repair_cost: $0 (cleaning/adjustment), $30-80 damper parts, $80-200 servo motor, $100-300 with labor
rower.notes: Concept2 air rowers: vacuum the flywheel cage and fan blades — dust buildup is the #1 cause of resistance issues. Check damper lever moves freely. WaterRower: check water level and add purification tablets. Magnetic: test wiring to servo motor. Gritty feel on Concept2 means clutch lubrication is contaminated.

rower.symptom: Seat sticks or jerks during rowing, rough sliding motion, wobbling seat, grinding noise from seat area
rower.root_cause: Worn seat rollers developing flat spots, dirty/debris-covered rail, or bent monorail from impact
rower.notes: Clean monorail with damp cloth monthly. Mist seat rollers with silicone spray then wipe rail clean. Concept2 sells replacement roller kits. Loose footplate screws cause 40% of seat misalignment issues — check footplates first. Flat spots develop from sitting in one position.

rower.symptom: Chain skipping or running roughly, cord fraying visually, handle doesn't retract smoothly, snapping noise during pull
rower.root_cause: Chain link stiffness from lack of lubrication, cord wear from repeated bending, or sprocket wear
rower.notes: SAFETY: Inspect chain/cord regularly — a broken chain or cord mid-pull can cause injury. Concept2 chains: lubricate with Teflon oil (5 drops max), check for stiff links by rowing slowly and watching chain flex around sprocket. If chain measures over 12.5 inches across 12 links, it's stretched and needs replacement. WaterRower uses a strap/cord system that eventually needs replacement.

rower.symptom: Monitor dead or intermittent, display segments missing, inaccurate stroke count or distance, monitor resets during rowing
rower.root_cause: Battery terminal corrosion from sweat exposure (60% of display failures), failed pickup sensor, or internal board damage
rower.notes: Sweat corrosion on battery terminals causes 60% of monitor failures. Clean contacts with cotton swab dipped in vinegar. Remove batteries when not in use for extended periods. Concept2 PM5 monitors are expensive ($200) but very repairable — sensor wire and battery contacts are the usual culprits. Check pickup wire connection to flywheel sensor.

rower.symptom: Foot strap won't hold tension, strap buckle broken, foot plate cracked, heel cup broken
rower.root_cause: Strap material degradation from sweat and UV, buckle mechanism wear, or foot plate fatigue cracking from repeated flex
rower.estimated_repair_cost: $10-25 straps DIY, $20-50 foot plates, $60-100 with labor
rower.notes: Straps are consumable items. Concept2 straps are cheap and snap-in replacement. Foot plates on budget rowers crack from repeated flexing — especially at the hinge point. Loose footplate screws cause downstream seat alignment issues.

rower.symptom: Handle doesn't retract fully, handle hangs loose at full extension, slow retraction speed
rower.root_cause: Bungee/shock cord stretching from repeated use and age, or cold temperature reducing elasticity
rower.notes: Concept2 shock cord replacement is a well-documented DIY repair. Remove old cord, thread new cord following the routing path, and tension appropriately. Cold rooms cause temporary loss of elasticity — try warming the room before replacing. Order manufacturer-specific cord for correct length and tension.

rower.symptom: Handle pulls but no resistance generated, flywheel spins freely in both directions, slipping feel during the drive phase
rower.root_cause: One-way clutch/bearing failure preventing flywheel engagement, contaminated or dried-out clutch lubricant
rower.notes: The one-way clutch allows the flywheel to spin freely on the recovery but engage on the drive. If lubricant dries out or gets contaminated, the clutch won't engage. Concept2 sells a balanced flywheel assembly with pre-installed clutch. This is a less common but workout-ending failure.

rower.symptom: Water leaking from tank, water level dropping without visible leak, algae or discoloration in water
rower.root_cause: Tank seal degradation, stopper plug failure, or micro-cracks in polycarbonate tank from UV exposure or age
rower.notes: WaterRower-specific issue. Add purification tablets every 6 months to prevent algae. Check stopper plug tightness. If tank is cracked, replacement is the only option. Keep out of direct sunlight to prevent UV damage to polycarbonate. Water level directly affects resistance — refill per manufacturer markings.

cable_machine.symptom: Visible wire strands poking out of cable, rough feel when pulling cable, cable appears fuzzy or thinning at any point
cable_machine.root_cause: Fatigue failure from repeated bending cycles around pulleys, sweat corrosion, pulley misalignment causing uneven wear, or age (manufacturers recommend replacement every 2-3 years)
cable_machine.estimated_repair_cost: $30-80 cable DIY, $199 flat rate at many shops, $150-300 with labor
cable_machine.notes: CRITICAL SAFETY ISSUE. A snapped cable can cause the weight stack to drop from height, causing severe injury. Inspect cables monthly — run a cloth along the full length; any snagging means replacement. NEVER use a cable showing ANY fraying. Lock out the machine immediately. Cable failure has caused significant injury lawsuits. Replace cables every 2-3 years even if they look OK.

cable_machine.symptom: Grinding or squeaking noise during cable movement, jerky cable motion, cable jumping off pulley track, pulley not spinning freely
cable_machine.root_cause: Bearing wear from lack of lubrication, dust/dirt contamination, or moisture from sweat and humidity
cable_machine.estimated_repair_cost: $10-30 bearings DIY, $80-150 with labor per pulley
cable_machine.notes: Bad pulleys cause uneven cable wear and accelerate cable fraying. Most use standard sealed bearings — note the bearing number and order replacements. A pulley that doesn't spin freely puts asymmetric load on the cable. Clean and inspect all pulleys when replacing a cable. Lubricate every 3-6 months.

cable_machine.symptom: Pin won't fully engage, weight plate slides off during lift, pin bent or won't slide in, clicking noise from weight stack
cable_machine.root_cause: Pin bending from being dropped or forced at an angle, guide hole wear, or debris in the pin channel
cable_machine.notes: SAFETY: A pin that doesn't fully engage can allow weights to drop during a lift. Inspect pin for bending — replace if not perfectly straight. Clean pin holes regularly. Universal replacement pins are available but check diameter and length. Magnetic selector pins provide more reliable engagement.

cable_machine.symptom: Cable jumped off pulley, cable rubbing on frame, uneven resistance through range of motion, cable crossed over itself
cable_machine.root_cause: Cable jumped its track from sudden release of tension (dropping weight stack), worn pulley groove, or improper cable replacement
cable_machine.notes: Take photos of cable routing BEFORE any cable work — routing through multiple pulleys is complex and easy to get wrong. A derailed cable rubbing on the frame will fray quickly. Check pulley groove wear — a worn groove allows cable to jump. Never allow weight stack to slam down — this is the primary cause of cable derailment.

cable_machine.symptom: Weight stack sticking during lift, grinding noise from weight stack, uneven plate movement, plates tilting
cable_machine.root_cause: Guide rod corrosion from sweat and humidity, lack of lubrication, bent guide rod from impact, or guide bushing wear in weight plates
cable_machine.estimated_repair_cost: $0 (lubricate), $30-60 bushings, $80-200 guide rod replacement with labor
cable_machine.notes: Lubricate guide rods with silicone spray every 1-2 months. Wipe down after lubricating to prevent dust attraction. If rod is bent, it must be replaced — do not attempt to straighten. Check nylon guide bushings in each weight plate — worn bushings cause tilting and sticking.

cable_machine.symptom: Wobbling or instability during use, clicking from frame joints, visible gap between frame members, bolts found on floor
cable_machine.root_cause: Hardware loosening from vibration over time, especially on bolted (non-welded) frame connections
cable_machine.notes: SAFETY: Loose frame hardware under heavy load is dangerous. Check all bolts quarterly with appropriate wrench/socket. Apply threadlocker (blue Loctite) to bolts that repeatedly loosen. Home gym equipment with bolted frames needs more frequent checks than welded commercial equipment.

cable_machine.symptom: Carabiner gate won't close fully, swivel seized and won't rotate, attachment disconnects during use
cable_machine.root_cause: Spring fatigue in carabiner gate, sweat corrosion seizing swivel, or worn latch mechanism
cable_machine.notes: SAFETY: A carabiner that won't fully close can release an attachment under load. Replace immediately if gate spring is weak or latch is worn. Use rated gym carabiners, not hardware store clips. Swivel joints seize from sweat — apply light oil periodically. Inspect before every use.

cable_machine.symptom: Cable pulling out of end fitting, loose ball/stop at cable end, cable end fraying near the ferrule
cable_machine.root_cause: Crimped ferrule loosening from repeated stress cycles, or corrosion weakening the cable strands at the ferrule junction
cable_machine.notes: SAFETY: A cable pulling free from its termination allows weights to drop instantly. If any movement is detected at a ferrule/end fitting, stop using immediately and replace the entire cable. This is not a field-repairable component — the cable must be factory-terminated or replaced.

smith_machine.symptom: Bar sticks or grinds during movement, bar doesn't slide smoothly, visible scoring on guide rods, uneven feel between sides
smith_machine.root_cause: Linear bearing wear from lack of lubrication, dust and chalk contamination, or guide rod surface damage
smith_machine.estimated_repair_cost: $20-60 bearings DIY, $50-120 with lubricant and bearings, $150-300 with labor
smith_machine.notes: Lubricate guide rods with silicone-based lubricant regularly — this is the #1 maintenance item. Most sticking resolves with proper lubrication. If guide rods are scored, linear bearings will wear prematurely regardless of lubrication. Bronze bushings on budget models wear faster than ball-bearing linear slides.

smith_machine.symptom: Safety catches won't engage at some positions, bar doesn't lock when rotated, catches feel loose or don't hold under load
smith_machine.root_cause: Safety hook wear from repeated engagement, spring fatigue in auto-locking mechanisms, or debris in catch slots
smith_machine.notes: CRITICAL SAFETY: Test safety catches under load before every heavy session. Clean catch slots of chalk and debris. Inspect hook faces for rounding or wear — replace if hooks don't seat firmly. J-hooks and safety catches are the primary safety system on a Smith machine. Never use with compromised catches.

smith_machine.symptom: Cable fraying, pulley grinding, uneven cable pull, cable jumped off track
smith_machine.root_cause: Same failure modes as standalone cable machines — cable fatigue, pulley bearing wear, and routing issues
smith_machine.notes: Many home Smith machines include cable crossover stations. Same safety concerns as standalone cable machines — inspect cables monthly, replace at first sign of fraying. Multi-function home gyms have complex cable routing — photograph before disassembly. Budget combo units use thinner cables that wear faster.

smith_machine.symptom: Plates won't slide on/off easily, peg bent or wobbling, weight plates shifting during movement
smith_machine.root_cause: Peg bending from uneven loading or impact, weld point fatigue where peg meets frame, or plate hole burrs catching
smith_machine.notes: Bent pegs are usually caused by loading weight on one side only or dropping loaded bar onto pegs. Check peg straightness by rolling on flat surface. Welded pegs that break require re-welding or frame repair. Bolt-on pegs are easier to replace. Use spring collars to prevent plate shifting.

smith_machine.symptom: Wobbling or racking movement, clicking sounds from frame joints, visible gaps at bolt junctions, bolts found on floor
smith_machine.root_cause: Bolt loosening from vibration and repeated loading/unloading cycles, especially on bolt-together frames
smith_machine.notes: SAFETY: Check ALL frame bolts monthly. Smith machines transmit significant load through frame joints. Apply blue Loctite threadlocker to bolts that repeatedly loosen. Do not use red (permanent) Loctite — blue allows future disassembly. Bolt-together home gyms need more frequent checks than welded commercial units.

smith_machine.symptom: Bar feels excessively heavy at starting position, uneven bar weight through range, gas strut leaking, springs losing tension
smith_machine.root_cause: Gas strut seal failure causing pressure loss, spring fatigue from years of cycling, or counter-balance cable wear
smith_machine.notes: Counter-balanced Smith machines use gas struts or springs to offset the bar weight. Gas struts have a finite lifespan and are not repairable — replace when they lose pressure. Springs lose tension gradually and may need periodic replacement. Some models use a weight-based counterbalance that requires cable maintenance.

smith_machine.symptom: Bar won't rotate smoothly for hook engagement, inconsistent lock/unlock feel, bar gets stuck in locked position
smith_machine.root_cause: Rotation bushing wear, debris in rotation channel, or hook geometry worn from repeated use
smith_machine.notes: The twist-to-lock mechanism is essential for safe Smith machine use. Lubricate rotation points with silicone. Check that hooks on both sides engage simultaneously. If one side catches before the other, the mechanism is misaligned and needs adjustment. Some models have adjustable hook depth.

smith_machine.symptom: J-hooks won't stay at set height, safeties drop under load, adjustment pin bent or missing
smith_machine.root_cause: Pop-pin spring fatigue, hole enlargement in uprights from repeated use, or j-hook face wear
smith_machine.notes: SAFETY: J-hooks and safety arms must hold rated load without slipping. Test under load before heavy lifts. Inspect pin engagement — pin should fully seat through both holes. Replace j-hooks if face is worn or deformed. Westside hole spacing (1 inch) provides more precise positioning than 2-inch spacing.

spin_bike.symptom: Squealing noise when resistance applied, uneven braking feel, resistance knob turns but no change in difficulty, burning smell
spin_bike.root_cause: Felt/leather brake pad wear from friction against flywheel, pad glazing, or pad holder misalignment
spin_bike.notes: Felt pads are consumable items on friction-resistance bikes. Replace when thin, glazed (shiny), or uneven. Emergency brake (push-down resistance knob) is also the resistance control on many bikes — pad wears faster from emergency stops. Magnetic resistance bikes don't have this issue. Silicone spray on the pad can temporarily reduce squealing.

spin_bike.symptom: Grinding, clicking, or rubbing noise that gets louder with speed, rough/gritty feel when pedaling, vibration through frame
spin_bike.root_cause: Bearing wear accelerated by sweat exposure (sweat drips directly onto flywheel housing on many spin bikes), dust, and high-RPM stress
spin_bike.notes: Peloton bikes are especially prone — flywheel has 3 bearings (2 large on belt side, 1 small on opposite side). Bearings last 12-24 months at 15 miles/day. Characteristic click-click-click sound like a playing card in bicycle spokes. Standard sealed bearings — note the number stamped on them. Sweat mat and regular wiping prevent premature failure.

spin_bike.symptom: Cleat won't clip in or out, pedal feels loose when clipped in, clicking during pedal stroke, toe cage straps broken
spin_bike.root_cause: Cleat mechanism wear (SPD, Look Delta, or toe cage), pedal bearing failure, or cleat bolts loosened on shoe
spin_bike.estimated_repair_cost: $15-50 pedals DIY (toe cage), $30-100 clipless pedals, $80-150 with labor
spin_bike.notes: Peloton uses Look Delta cleats; most commercial spin bikes use SPD. Cleats on shoes wear out and should be replaced every 6-12 months. Left pedal is reverse-threaded. Toe cage straps break frequently on budget bikes — upgrade to dual-sided SPD/flat pedals. Pedal tension spring can be adjusted for easier clip-in/out.

spin_bike.symptom: Handlebar drops during riding, height adjustment won't hold, handlebar wobbles side to side, creaking during climbing position
spin_bike.root_cause: Pop-pin mechanism wear, knob clamp insufficient pressure, handlebar post corrosion from sweat, or stripped adjustment threads
spin_bike.notes: Spin bikes see extreme handlebar stress during standing climbs. Grease the handlebar post during assembly to prevent corrosion seizure. Carbon paste (from bicycle industry) improves grip on adjustment posts. Check clamp bolt torque periodically. Sweat running down arms into adjustment mechanism is the primary corrosion source.

spin_bike.symptom: Seat drops during riding, seat won't stay at set height, seat fore/aft adjustment slips, clicking from seat area
spin_bike.root_cause: Same corrosion and clamp issues as handlebar — worsened by direct sweat contact from seated position
spin_bike.notes: The single most annoying spin bike issue. Seat post gets the most sweat exposure of any component. Grease post and clamp surfaces. Tighten clamp bolt firmly — not enough to strip, but more than hand-tight. Pop-pin designs are more reliable than knob/clamp. If post is stuck from corrosion, penetrating oil and gentle twisting.

spin_bike.symptom: Clicking or ticking noise at regular intervals, slipping under hard effort, visible wear or cracks on belt, chain noise or rust
spin_bike.root_cause: Belt stretch over time, missing belt teeth, chain stretch from lack of lubrication, or tension adjustment needed
spin_bike.estimated_repair_cost: $20-50 belt or chain DIY, $80-150 with labor
spin_bike.notes: Belt-drive spin bikes are quieter and lower maintenance. Check belt for cracks, missing teeth, or glazing. Chain-drive bikes need regular lubrication (same as bicycle chain). Measure old belt/chain exactly before ordering replacement — length and width matter. A slipping belt under hard effort can cause sudden loss of resistance — potential injury.

spin_bike.symptom: Console intermittently dies, Bluetooth/WiFi connectivity lost, touchscreen unresponsive in some areas, corrosion visible on frame near electronics
spin_bike.root_cause: Sweat is corrosive (salt + moisture) and spin bikes generate extreme sweat volumes that drip directly onto the frame, electronics, and internal components
spin_bike.notes: UNIQUE TO SPIN BIKES — sweat damage is the #1 hidden killer. Place a towel over the handlebar/frame during every ride. Wipe down immediately after every ride — never let sweat dry on the bike. Peloton screen failures, bearing failures, and frame corrosion are overwhelmingly sweat-related. Use a sweat mat on the floor. A fan reduces dripping. Sweat guards are available for most models.

spin_bike.symptom: Creaking or clicking centered at crank junction, rough pedal rotation when spun by hand, lateral wobble in crank arms
spin_bike.root_cause: Bottom bracket bearing wear from sweat dripping down seat post into bracket area, high-torque spinning loads
spin_bike.notes: Spin bike bottom brackets take more abuse than regular exercise bikes due to standing climbs and high-torque intervals. Many use standard bicycle bottom bracket sizes. Requires crank puller and BB tool. Replace both sides. Sweat guard installation on the seat post helps prevent sweat from reaching the BB.
